title: CTE — break complex queries into readable steps
instruction:
This query would be a deeply nested mess without CTEs. With CTEs, each step has a name and the final query reads like a story.

Calculate which departments are over budget by comparing actual payroll to their budget.
starter:
WITH dept_payroll AS (
  SELECT
    department_id,
    SUM(salary) AS total_payroll,
    COUNT(*) AS headcount
  FROM employees
  GROUP BY department_id
)
SELECT
  d.name                                   AS department,
  dp.headcount,
  dp.total_payroll,
  d.budget,
  ROUND(dp.total_payroll * 100.0 / d.budget, 1) AS payroll_pct
FROM dept_payroll dp
JOIN departments d ON d.id = dp.department_id
ORDER BY payroll_pct DESC;
successMessage: Engineering's payroll is $668k against a $2.4M budget (27.8%) — there's room to hire. Data Science is at 20.8% — they have significant budget capacity. CTEs let you compute `dept_payroll` once and reference it clearly.

title: Multiple CTEs — a complete revenue report
instruction:
Chain multiple CTEs to build up analysis step by step. Each CTE can reference the ones before it.

Build a full customer health report: total revenue, order count, and average deal size.
starter:
WITH paid_orders AS (
  SELECT customer_id, amount
  FROM orders
  WHERE status = 'paid'
),
customer_revenue AS (
  SELECT
    customer_id,
    COUNT(*)           AS orders,
    SUM(amount)        AS revenue,
    ROUND(AVG(amount)) AS avg_deal
  FROM paid_orders
  GROUP BY customer_id
),
top_customers AS (
  SELECT * FROM customer_revenue
  WHERE revenue >= 24000
)
SELECT
  c.name,
  c.country,
  tc.orders,
  tc.revenue,
  tc.avg_deal
FROM top_customers tc
JOIN customers c ON c.id = tc.customer_id
ORDER BY tc.revenue DESC;
successMessage: Three CTEs building on each other: `paid_orders` filters, `customer_revenue` aggregates, `top_customers` filters to high-value accounts. Momentum Co is the top customer with 3 Enterprise Plan renewals. Try lowering the $24k threshold to see more customers.

title: Recursive CTE — generate a date range
instruction:
Recursive CTEs can generate sequences. This is essential for time-series reports — if you want revenue-per-day, you need a complete list of dates even for days with no orders.

Generate the first 7 days of 2024 to simulate a date spine.
starter:
WITH RECURSIVE date_spine(day_num, day_label) AS (
  SELECT 1, '2024-01-01'
  UNION ALL
  SELECT
    day_num + 1,
    DATE('2024-01-01', '+' || day_num || ' days')
  FROM date_spine
  WHERE day_num < 7
)
SELECT
  day_num,
  day_label,
  CASE CAST(strftime('%w', day_label) AS INTEGER)
    WHEN 0 THEN 'Sunday'
    WHEN 1 THEN 'Monday'
    WHEN 2 THEN 'Tuesday'
    WHEN 3 THEN 'Wednesday'
    WHEN 4 THEN 'Thursday'
    WHEN 5 THEN 'Friday'
    WHEN 6 THEN 'Saturday'
  END AS weekday
FROM date_spine;
successMessage: A 7-day date spine. In real analytics, you LEFT JOIN this against your orders table to fill in zeros for days with no revenue — giving you a complete time-series instead of gaps. Recursive CTEs are essential for this pattern.
